By Shawn Twing
Google (search) is about answers to questions, and those
questions often appear as problems or solutions. For example, “�x
a leaky roof” is a problem-focused search query.
“Find a roo�ng contractor near me,” is a solution-focused search
query. Both are addressing the same underlying issue, but from
very different perspectives.
Other examples:
▪ “how to lose weight” (problem) vs. “best diets 2018” (solution).
▪ “lower back pain” (problem) vs. “chiropractors near me”
(solution).
The key to understanding Google search is that these queries
expresses ACTIVE INTENT. Your Google search ads only appear
when someone has actively requested them.
The Typical Approach
The Problem/Solution Mindset
(Advertising on Google Ads)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 17 --

The basic template for a conventional Google search ad is:
�. con�rming the problem,
�. agitating the problem,
�. and then offering a solution to the problem.
For example, let’s assume that you are a business coach who helps
small businesses improve cash �ow.
You have found a vein of search traf�c for “how do I improve my
business cash �ow”, and you decide to advertise on Google
AdWords (search) to �nd new prospects.
Your ad — and most of your competitors’ ads — will generally
follow the same basic format:
▪ Headline: Improve Your Business Cash Flow. (I have con�rmed
that what you’re looking for and what I o�er are in sync.)
▪ Ad copy: Tired of struggling with your businesses cash �ow?
Concerned it’s only going to get worse? (And now I’m poking the
problem even more — “Wait, what, it can get worse?”)
▪ Call to action: The XYZ method has helped thousands of people
just like you … learn more. (All you have to do is click here and you’re
on your way to solving your problems…)
This format works — and it has worked for a long time. The
problem is that it has worked so well almost EVERYONE uses
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 17 --

that same approach.
The SOI Approach
A multi-page presell site offers an alternative, especially if we’ve
established a new frame of reference with our worldbuilding.
I like to think about these alternatives in two broad strategies. The
�rst I call “Did you know?”, and the second is “What insiders
know that you don’t.” They’re similar, and each can be tweaked
to �t your particular business strategy.
The “Did You Know?” Strategy
The world you’re building starts with an ad, and it’s an incredibly
effective way to build trust and rapport early.
It’s also a way to tilt the conversation entirely in your favor.
Let’s start with “did you know?”
Imagine the same scenario again — a (real, live) person has
searched Google for “how do I improve my business cash �ow?”,
and three ads appear.
Two of those ads are very similar to the ad described above — each
af�rms, agitates, then offers a solution to the problem. The
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 17 --

third ad, however, stands out:
▪ Headline: Eliminate Cash Flow Problems at the Source
▪ Ad copy: Did you know most cash �ow problems are actually
business model problems?
▪ Call to action: Three simple changes to improve your cash �ow
immediately.
This ad stands out because it’s different.
That makes it immediately attractive. It also establishes an
entirely different world view.
Cash �ow isn’t the problem — cash �ow is a symptom of the
problem. The real problem is in your business model, and there
are some simple changes you can make immediately.
That’s a change in perspective that can be an entry point into
a multi-page presell site / SOI-inspired world.
“Did you know?” has many variations (and no, you don’t actually
need to include “did you know” in the ad copy).
It’s an organizing idea, a way of framing a conversation for a
prospect at the point of �rst contact in a way that immediately
differentiates your ads from your competitors’ ads.
It’s the �rst signal that you’re different. You and your competitors
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 17 --

are bidding on the same keyword phrases, but you’re establishing
yourself in a completely different way.
The “What Insiders Know” Strategy
OK, let’s move on to the next AdWords ad copy strategy — insider
knowledge.
People love secrets. (Rich Schefren describes secrets as a form of
“social currency” — I love that.)
Think about the last time you knew something no one else did
and were about to share that with someone. How exciting does
that feel?
We can replicate this idea with our Google search strategy by
suggesting that there are things that insiders know that outsiders
don’t.
Is this true? Of course it is — the reasons insiders are insiders is
because they know the things that outsiders don’t!
However, we can put our own stake in the ground when we offer
to share the insider secrets with the outsiders.
Let’s use our cash �ow search again as an example.
Our prospect has searched for “how do I improve my business
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 17 --

cash �ow.” Your ad looks like this:
▪ Headline: The Truth About Cash Flow
▪ Ad copy: Five things your accountant won’t tell you.
▪ Call to action: Learn what the pros know with our free guide.
There are SO many ways to use this approach to establish a frame.
It’s a strategy that can inform an entire MPPS.
I know I said I’d share two strategies but I want to overdeliver so
I’m going to share a third.
This is high-level ninja stuff and it’s a little more complicated
(and a LOT more effective). It works with ad copy as an entry
point but the real power is in the rest of the worldview built
around the idea.
The “I Wouldn’t Do it That Way
Because…” Strategy
Here’s the basic idea.
I’m assuming that you understand how your competitors deliver
value and that the way you deliver value is reasonably similar.
Using our cash �ow coaching example above, let’s assume that
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 17 --

you and your top two competitors all require a prospect to �ll out
a lengthy form describing the last six months of their cash �ow
history which you analyze before scheduling a follow up call
where you then explain how you could improve their cash �ow
problems, followed by a description of your pricing.
That’s the way everybody does it.
Here’s how to use the “I wouldn’t do it that way” strategy in this
example.
First, you need to mention casually (without drawing undue
attention) that almost everyone in your industry does things the
same way. That might sound like this:
“Most cash �ow consultants require 6-12 months of business data, a week
or two for analysis, and then a 1-2 hour call to go over the results.”
Then, you reinforce your authority, experience, and humility by
admitting you used to do the same thing:
“I understand why they do it — in fact, I did it that way for many years
too.”
Then, you pivot:
“However, a few years ago I realized that my clients came to me because
they needed results RIGHT NOW, so I changed my process entirely. Now I
start with a 60-minute phone call where you and I will discuss your most
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 17 --

di�cult cash �ow problem to make sure I can help you and to see how we
work together.”
Then, the �nal transformation—
“Don’t get me wrong — I think it’s a great idea to do the in-depth research
and solve cash �ow problems at the source, and that’s something we
would do after working on your most immediate problem. But right now
the priority is solving your most immediate challenge.”
I used to do it that way but now I don’t … which is really a way of
saying “I used to do it the way everyone still does it but now I
don’t — I’m new, different, better — than everyone else…”
Except you don’t have to say any of those things — the prospect
will be saying that to themselves for you!
Why is this so powerful?
It establishes a frame where every competitor your prospect has
talked to (or may talk to) does something that you have
established is less valuable to the prospect!
So how to we apply this to Google AdWords search advertising?
Something like this:
▪ Headline: The Cash Flow Results Method
▪ Ad Copy: Find your most important opportunity in 60 minutes or
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 17 --

less.
▪ Call to Action: Discover the simple change that skyrocketed our
clients’ results.
Putting it All in Action
Each of the strategies — “did you know?”, “I have a secret…”, and “I
wouldn’t do it that way.” — establish powerful frames to enter into
a beliefs-focused multi-page presell site.
Because you’re advertising on AdWords you know that someone
has searched, which means they’re actively looking.
You also know that you have competitors, most of whom will be
using the same basic formula and saying the same basic thing: I
know what you’re looking for, it’s a big problem, and I know how
to solve it.
What you’re doing, instead, is taking control over the entire view
of the world that the prospect has. You are establishing the frame
of reference through which a prospect will see you (and evaluate
your competitors).
“Did you know?” leads with value. Rather than try to agitate the
problem, you’re offering a powerful idea immediately.
“I have a secret…” leads with intrigue. It is a very powerful
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 17 --

position to be in when you’re the person who showed someone
outside a world what the inside really looks like.
“I wouldn’t do it that way,” immediately de�nes you in a way
that’s superior to all of your competitors. I used to do things
the way everyone else still does them, but then I realized there’s a
better way…
Which is the best way for your business?
Test them and �nd out. Testing on AdWords is an inexpensive
way to answer this question.
Create a very basic, reasonably short landing page that explains
what you do and how you do it.
Don’t worry about all the details at this stage.
Your aim is to test which one of the hooks you plan to use is
actually interesting/engaging enough to get someone from an ad
to the landing page. Without investing time to write the entire
MPPS with that hook before you know if it works.
Then, use Google ads to test ideas. You can try “did you know?” vs.
“I have a secret”, or try different “did you know?” ideas. The sky’s
the limit.
One word of warning — you’re not looking for statistical
signi�cance.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 17 --

You’re gathering anecdotal data.
If one ad has a 7% click through rate and the other has an 8% click
through rate after 100 clicks there isn’t a real difference.
But if one ad has a 25% click through rate and the other has a 5%
CTR after 100 clicks, you know which approach resonates with
your audience. Experiment, test ideas, get feedback, and then use
that information to write more engaging Multi Page Presell Site
content.
Don’t go overboard with testing, however.
It can be a form of procrastination. Develop your MPPS idea and
then brainstorm 2-3 different entry points for search traf�c. Then
test those 2-3, declare a winner, and move on. If you have several
ideas for an overall theme for your MPPS, then test those themes
quickly and GET WRITING.
The value in AdWords testing data for our purposes is in the
extremes.
If you have an idea for an MPPS theme, write a page or two of
content and create a search ad based on that theme. If those ads
get very little response, take a step back and reconsider the theme
(assuming it’s a good match to the search traf�c you’re visible for).
If, on the other hand, you have an idea for an MPPS theme, create
search ads for that, and get a 20%+ click through rate, you’re on to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 17 --

something and should move forward with it quickly.
Advanced Considerations to Optimize
Your Ads’ Performance
OK, switching gears. Let’s talk about some factors that can affect
search performance and how to use those factors to our
advantage.
First: Determine your perfect search criteria.
The �rst determination we need to make is how are we going to
de�ne a perfect search? The factors we need to consider are the
search phrase itself and the physical location of the person
making the search. I’ll use an example to explain what I mean.
Several years ago I worked with a client who owned a writer’s
retreat not far from my home in central Vermont.
There’s a reasonable amount of search traf�c for variations of
searches that express interest in writer/writing retreats.
Some of those searches include geographic intent (e.g., “writers
retreat Philadelphia, PA”). Other searches included seasonality
(teachers often attend writing retreats when they’re not in school)
— for example, “writing retreats summer 2018”.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 17 --

The �rst thing we had to do was setup the basic structure of the
account so it would include the high value searches we wanted to
be visible for (variations of writers and writing retreats), while
also preventing searches we knew weren’t high value using
negative keywords.
However, even after we eliminated all of the keyword signals that
indicated a less than perfect search, we still didn’t have a perfect
audience.
The next step was using geography in our favor.
There are hundreds of searches per day for writers and writing
retreats that met my client’s criteria. However, a search for a
writing retreat by someone in California (7+ hour �ight away)
was far less likely to convert to a customer than a search for a
writing retreat by someone in Connecticut (2-3 hours’ drive away).
Knowing that, we setup the client’s account to bid on searches for
their ideal search term by people who could drive to the location
in �ve hours or less. That became our “perfect” search criteria.
When ads and offers underperformed, we knew the audience was
right, which meant we could focus our attention in other areas.
Your business will have a different set of criteria, but I want you
to think about it the same way.
▪ What is the combination of factors that would make a real,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 17 --

live human being performing a search perfect for your
business?
▪ What was that speci�c search phrase?
▪ What wasn’t included in that search that would
disqualify it?
▪ And are there geographic or other factors that you could
use to stack the deck in your favor?
That’s your starting point for using AdWords. That’s what you’ll
use to create a solid foundation. Next you’ll use your own
variation of “did you know?”, “insider’s secret”, or “I wouldn’t do
that…”. Test and see what works best for you.
The MPPS format will give you several advantages.
As I mentioned in the previous lesson, Google rewards quality
advertising — as measured by user engagement — with lower bid
prices and better ad positions. A multi-page presell site is built to
be engaging, interesting, and to pull readers forward page by page
’til the end.
That translates into low bounce rates, high time on site, and
high number of average pageviews — three factors that tell
Google your advertising is RELEVANT!
Second: Consider your prospect’s intent.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 17 --

One word of warning with AdWords which may or may not be
true in your market (so please test this!).
Very speci�c search queries often do better with shorter presell
sites. In general, the more speci�c the search term the more
actively your prospect is looking for an immediate solution. Too
much content can be a barrier to that.
There is no cookie-cutter solution to this, and the solution for
you will depend on your unique circumstances.
If you’re planning to use AdWords for the majority if your paid
traf�c, I would suggest starting with a three-page Presell Site (PS).
Over time, experimenting to see if you get better performance
with a two-page version, depending on search intent.
These are suggestions, not rules. If you are establishing yourself
as an alternative to conventional wisdom, and that requires
signi�cant explanation and belief-building, then you may need to
use a longer MPPS for AdWords search.
Whatever works is the best solution.
Thirdly: Congruence matters.
One �nal word of advice on AdWords and SOI — pay attention to
congruence between your ads and the pages you lead people to
from those ads.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 17 --

The Google ad becomes the point of entry into the world you’re
building, and that point of entry needs to be the �rst step into that
world.
The goal isn’t to trick someone with a clever ad and then share
your SOI-inspired worldview. The goal is to offer a new way of
thinking about something, and the ad teases that / opens the door
to that.
The content in the MPPS must deliver on the implicit promise of
the ad copy or the mismatch will depress performance.
Lastly: Your ad must be your gatekeeper.
Create your ad messaging to �lter out the wrong audience as
much as you’re trying to �lter in the right audience.
I’m going to talk speci�cally about how we can use the
advertising platform to do this in a later lesson. For now it’s
enough to think about who do you want to engage with,
speci�cally. And who do you NOT want to pay for, to get to your
PS?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 17 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 17 --
